title: Recycling and reusing glass correctly
subtitle: Which glass types are recyclable, which are not, and how to maximise reuse before disposal
excerpt: Glass bottles and jars are 100 percent recyclable without quality loss and can be recycled indefinitely. However, not all glass is the same: window glass, Pyrex ovenware, drinking glasses, and mirrors have different chemical compositions to container glass and contaminate the recycling stream if mixed in. UK glass recycling rates stand at around 68 percent of container glass; the main barrier is household uncertainty about what can and cannot go in the bottle bank.
sourceNotes: British Glass statistics on glass recycling rates 2024. WRAP Glass Recycling guidance 2024. European Container Glass Federation (FEVE) on glass composition differences. Recycle Now guidance on non-container glass disposal.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soda-lime glass used to manufacture bottles and jars. The primary glass type accepted in kerbside recycling bins and bottle banks. Made from silica sand, soda ash, limestone, and recycled cullet. Recyclable indefinitely without loss of quality. Melts at approximately 1,500 degrees Celsius in a bottle furnace.

A heat-resistant glass containing boron trioxide, used in ovenware (Pyrex), laboratory equipment, and some baking dishes. Has a higher melting point and different thermal expansion coefficient to container glass. If mixed into container glass recycling, it creates weak spots in new bottles. Not accepted in kerbside recycling or bottle banks.

Crushed or broken glass used as a raw material in new glass manufacture. Each tonne of cullet used saves 1.2 tonnes of raw materials (sand, soda ash, limestone) and reduces energy consumption by 2 to 3 percent compared to making glass from virgin raw materials. Using 50 percent cullet reduces CO2 emissions per tonne of glass by around 15 percent.

Flat glass made by floating molten glass on a bed of molten tin, used in windows, mirrors, and glazing. Has a different chemical composition to container glass; contains higher iron content and coatings that disrupt container glass recycling. Accepted at HWRC sites and by specialist glass processors; not accepted in kerbside bottle banks.

content.text: Glass bottles and jars can be recycled without losing quality, making them one of the most valuable items in a household recycling bin.
is sorted by colour and sent to bottle furnaces to be melted down for new bottles and jars. The rule for households is simple: bottles and jars go in the recycling; everything else goes elsewhere.

attrs.title: One wrong piece can contaminate a whole load

content.text: (a Pyrex dish or oven tray) mixed into a container glass batch causes weak spots in newly manufactured bottles when molten. Glass processors may reject or downgrade an entire batch if contamination is detected. At 1 in 500 pieces, borosilicate glass contaminant is detectable and causes problems. The rule is: if in doubt, take it to the HWRC rather than the bottle bank.

Bottles and jars (food, drink, sauces): kerbside recycling bin or bottle bank. Remove metal lids and recycle them separately with other metals. Labels do not need to be removed. A brief rinse is sufficient; perfectly clean glass is not required. These form the core

Window glass and glazing: HWRC glass/rubble bay. If a window is being replaced professionally, the installer typically takes the old glass. Broken window glass must be handled carefully; wrap in newspaper and label clearly before transporting.
: HWRC. Not accepted in bottle banks. Small decorative mirrors can be donated to charity shops if intact and in a frame.

Drinking glasses and glass tableware: charity shops if undamaged. Otherwise HWRC. Not in kerbside bins; the composition varies and many UK kerbside sorters do not separate it effectively from container glass.
Ovenware (Pyrex, baking dishes): charity shops or HWRC. Not in bottle banks.

Glass jars can be reused for years before recycling. A 500 ml jam jar works as a food storage vessel, fermentation container, or drinking glass for 5 to 10 years. Buying food in glass rather than plastic, where the jar has a practical second use, avoids buying new storage containers. One reused jam jar replaces the equivalent of 8 to 12 plastic containers over its useful life.

All food and drink bottles and jars go straight into the kerbside recycling bin, rinsed, lids removed. A set of 20 reused jam jars and pasta sauce jars stores dry goods, fermentation projects, and homemade preserves. When two drinking glasses broke, they were wrapped in newspaper, labelled (glass: not for recycling bin), and taken to the HWRC. A broken mirror from a renovation was similarly taken to the HWRC glass bay rather than the bottle bank.

A Pyrex baking dish that broke in the oven is placed in the kerbside glass bin. A broken mirror from a bathroom refit is placed in the bottle bank, along with a drinking glass that chipped. The household believes all glass is the same and can be recycled together. The contaminated batch at the recycling facility is downgraded from cullet to aggregate (road fill), and the recycling energy investment is largely wasted.

attrs.symptom: Kerbside recycling service does not accept glass
attrs.cause: Around 20 percent of UK councils do not include glass in kerbside collections, collecting only at bring banks
attrs.fix: Use the Recycle Now locator (recyclenow.com) to find the nearest bottle bank, which is typically at a supermarket car park or civic amenity site within 1 to 2 miles. Some councils offer glass collection from the kerbside on request; check the council website.

attrs.symptom: Large quantities of broken glass from a renovation need to be disposed of
attrs.cause: Renovation work generates flat glass and mixed glass debris that cannot go in kerbside bins
attrs.fix: Hire a skip or contact the HWRC to confirm they accept building glass in larger volumes. Some HWRCs have volume limits per visit. A glazing contractor disposing of old windows is classified as a controlled waste producer and may take the glass for a fee; ask when getting quotes for window replacement.

attrs.symptom: Glass jars accumulate faster than they can be reused
attrs.cause: Household generates more glass packaging than it can practically reuse
attrs.fix: Offer clean glass jars to local jam makers, fermenters, or craft groups via local Facebook groups or Freecycle. Local food banks sometimes accept clean glass jars for redistribution with homemade food donations. If still surplus, all container glass jars go in the kerbside recycling without guilt.
